text.title: css-in-js Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules for picking and operating CSS-in-JS in 2026 React stacks.

rule.statement: On React Server Components (Next 13+ App Router, Remix RSC), choose a zero-runtime library: vanilla-extract, Panda CSS, Linaria. Do NOT use emotion or styled-components in RSC trees.
rule.rationale: Runtime CSS-in-JS does not work in Server Components — the styles cannot be serialised across the server/client boundary, leading to FOUC or build errors. Zero-runtime libs extract styles at build time.

rule.statement: Runtime CSS-in-JS (emotion, styled-components) is acceptable ONLY in pure client SPAs without RSC.
rule.rationale: Runtime libraries provide ergonomic dynamic styling at the cost of runtime overhead. That cost is fine in client SPAs, prohibitive in RSC.

rule.statement: Declare `styled` components at module scope, never inside React render functions. Define once, reuse across renders.
rule.rationale: Defining styled inside render creates a new component class per render, defeating memoisation and triggering full re-mount of the styled subtree.

rule.statement: Use transient props (`$primary`) for styling props so they are not forwarded to the underlying DOM element.
rule.rationale: Non-transient styling props leak to the DOM as invalid HTML attributes and trigger React warnings. `$prefix` keeps them in JS land.

rule.statement: Augment the library's theme type via `declare module "styled-components" { export interface DefaultTheme extends MyTheme {} }` so `theme.color.primary` is type-safe.
rule.rationale: Without augmentation, `theme` is `any`; refactors silently break theme references. Augmentation makes the theme part of the type system.
